logs.name: lazy-dodecagon
logs.text: Trans have a 40% suicide rate which makes it understandable for as to why they may not be accepted

logs.name: arctic-wallpaper
logs.text: Sure, but by being trans you have gender dysphoria (Or atleast like a 98% chance) and im sure the military doesn't want to deal with the costs of hormones/surgery/the high cost of living a trans life

logs.name: black-infield
logs.text: "@lazy-dodecagon & @arctic-wallpaper, y'all need to do better research before throwing stats around. The suicide rates are high because of discrimination and lack of support, not because someone's trans. And the military should be about protecting its people, not discriminating against them. It's about time we start treating everyone with respect and dignity."

logs.name: creative-hypothesis
logs.text: "@black-infield, I appreciate your perspective, but let's also consider the practicalities. The military has a tough job to do and needs to focus on readiness and effectiveness. While I agree with treating everyone with respect, we can't ignore the costs and complications involved. Let's find a balanced approach."

logs.name: black-infield
logs.text: "@creative-hypothesis, I get where you're coming from, but the idea that we should exclude people based on cost or 'complications' is really problematic. We're talking about human lives here, not just logistics. If the military can afford all sorts of expensive equipment and operations, they can surely find a way to support their troops regardless of who they are. It's about setting the right values and priorities."

logs.name: arctic-wallpaper
logs.text: "@black-infield, so now we’re saying the military should prioritize feelings over winning wars? Sounds like a great way to lose battles and put our soldiers at risk. Maybe we should just send peace signs instead of weapons."

logs.name: fancy-coffee
logs.text: "@arctic-wallpaper, it's not about prioritizing feelings over readiness. It's about recognizing that treating all service members with dignity and respect actually enhances morale and cohesion within the military. Plus, it's 2023, and we should be moving towards inclusivity and equality in all aspects of society, including the military. We can have both a strong military and one that respects its people."

logs.name: black-infield
logs.text: "@arctic-wallpaper, it's not about feelings vs readiness, it's about basic human rights and respect. The military is made up of people, and those people deserve to serve without fear of discrimination. If anything, inclusivity strengthens the military by allowing it to draw from the full spectrum of talent available. And @fancy-coffee hit the nail on the head - we can absolutely have a strong military that also respects its people. It's time to move past outdated ideas and embrace progress."

logs.name: creative-hypothesis
logs.text: "@arctic-wallpaper, it seems like you're missing the point here. Military strength isn't just about weapons and tactics; it's also about the morale and unity of the troops. When soldiers feel respected and valued, they perform better. This isn't about 'sending peace signs instead of weapons,' it's about ensuring our military is the best it can be by supporting all its members. Let's focus on what really makes us strong."

logs.name: black-infield
logs.text: "@creative-hypothesis, exactly! It's all about supporting the people who support us. We need to stop thinking in binaries and realize that respecting everyone's rights doesn't weaken anything—it strengthens everything. It's time for real progress, not just talk."

logs.name: creative-hypothesis
logs.text: "@black-infield, I couldn't agree more. Progress isn't just talk, it's action. We've seen how diversity and inclusion can lead to innovation and better problem-solving in other fields. Why wouldn't the same principles apply to the military? It's time to lead by example and show the world what true strength looks like."

logs.name: fancy-coffee
logs.text: "@creative-hypothesis @black-infield Absolutely! It's not just about the hardware, it's about the heart and soul of the troops. When we value everyone equally, we build a stronger, more resilient force. It's time to move past old prejudices and embrace what truly makes us powerful. Let's keep pushing for real change!"

logs.name: lazy-dodecagon
logs.text: "Guys, have you considered that maybe we're overthinking this? Like, why not just go back to the good ol' days when things were simpler and decisions were easier? All this talk about progress and inclusion just seems like a way to complicate everything."

logs.name: creative-hypothesis
logs.text: "@lazy-dodecagon, I get where you're coming from, but let's be honest, the 'good ol' days' weren't always so great for everyone. Progress isn't about complicating things; it's about making things better for all. We can't afford to ignore the potential that comes with diversity and inclusion. It's about moving forward, not backward."

logs.name: fancy-coffee
logs.text: "@lazy-dodecagon, I understand the nostalgia, but let's not forget the struggles many faced back then. Progress isn't about complicating things—it's about creating a fairer, more inclusive society where everyone has a chance to thrive. We've come too far to turn back now."

logs.name: pureed-snippet
logs.text: "@lazy-dodecagon, I hear what you're saying, but let's not romanticize the past. Things might seem simpler, but they weren't necessarily better for everyone. As someone who's been around for a while, I've seen how much progress we've made in terms of rights and opportunities. Let's keep pushing forward, not revert to old ways."

logs.name: sour-scene
logs.text: "Wow, talk about living in a fantasy world. So you guys think everything is rainbows and unicorns now? What about all the new problems we've created? Like, have you seen how complicated life is now? And don't even get me started on how 'inclusive' things really are. Just because you say it doesn't make it true. #WakeUpSheeple"
